UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile information, linked to an external authentication system (e.g., Firebase Auth). This entity stores user-specific preferences and identifies the owner of tasks and schedules.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity. This typically corresponds to the user's ID from the external authentication system (e.g., Firebase Auth UID).

email.description: The email address associated with the user's account, primarily for display and identification. (From external authentication system)

displayName.description: The user's preferred display name or full name. (From external authentication system)

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was last updated.

Task.title: Task
Task.description: Represents a single task managed by the user, appearing on the Kanban board and potentially scheduled in the calendar view.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Task entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile entity that owns this task. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Task)

title.description: A concise title or name for the task.

description.description: A more detailed explanation or additional notes for the task.

status.description: The current state or progress of the task, corresponding to Kanban board columns.

context.description: The context (e.g., 'Work' or 'Study') to which this task belongs, used for filtering across the application.

dueDate.description: Optional date and time when the task is due, used for reminders and notifications.

scheduledStartTime.description: Optional start date and time if the task is explicitly scheduled in the weekly calendar view.

scheduledEndTime.description: Optional end date and time if the task is explicitly scheduled in the weekly calendar view.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the task was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the task was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The document ID (`userId`) must match the Firebase Authentication UID of the user. This path enforces owner-only access, ensuring `request.auth.uid` matches `{userId}`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user (Firebase Auth UID) who owns this profile.

definition.description: Stores tasks specific to a user. Each task document includes a denormalized `userId` field that MUST match the parent `userId` in the path and the authenticated user's ID. This ensures authorization independence for all read/write operations by allowing simple `request.auth.uid == userId && resource.data.userId == userId` checks.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user (Firebase Auth UID) who owns these tasks.

params.name: taskId
params.description: The unique ID of the task.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed following a user-centric, hierarchical model to maximize security, scalability, and debuggability, adhering strictly to the provided design principles. 

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):** Authorization independence is achieved by embedding the `userId` directly into the path of all user-specific data. For instance, user profiles are stored at `/users/{userId}`, and tasks are stored at `/users/{userId}/tasks/{taskId}`. Crucially, each `Task` document also includes a denormalized `userId` field. This allows Firestore Security Rules to perform simple, efficient checks like `request.auth.uid == userId` for the `/users/{userId}` collection and `request.auth.uid == userId && resource.data.userId == userId` for the `/users/{userId}/tasks/{taskId}` subcollection. This strategy completely eliminates the need for expensive and complex `get()` calls in security rules, which break atomic operations and are difficult to debug. It ensures that ownership is explicit and verifiable directly from the document's path and its internal fields.

**QAPs (Queries as Authorization Primitives):** The structural segregation enables secure `list` operations. A user can only 'list' tasks within their own `/users/{userId}/tasks` subcollection. The security rules will only permit reads if `request.auth.uid` matches the `{userId}` wildcard in the path. This means that any client-side query for tasks implicitly operates within the authenticated user's data scope, making `list` operations inherently secure. Filtering by task `status` ('Pending', 'Doing', 'Done') or `context` ('Work', 'Study') is then applied on the client-side or as additional `where` clauses within the already secured user's task subcollection, without requiring complex security rule logic to filter data.

**Structural Segregation:** Each collection and subcollection (`/users` and `/users/{userId}/tasks`) contains documents with a homogeneous security posture. The `/users` collection stores individual `UserProfile` documents, each accessible only by its owner. The `/users/{userId}/tasks` subcollection stores `Task` documents, all of which are owned by the parent `userId` and are therefore also only accessible by that user. This avoids mixing data with different access requirements, simplifying security rules significantly.

**Access Modeling & Consistency:** The design consistently uses path-based ownership (`/users/{userId}`) for private data and continues this hierarchy (`/users/{userId}/tasks/{taskId}`) for user-owned entities (Tasks). This pattern makes authorization highly predictable and easy to reason about. Wildcards like `{userId}` and `{taskId}` are descriptive, and fields like `userId` are consistently used for ownership tracking. The `Task` entity's `status` field explicitly models its state, avoiding ambiguous boolean flags.

This architecture minimizes rule complexity, maximizes security, and provides a clear, scalable foundation for the TaskMe application.
